ICSG forecasts secondary copper output boost

The international organization has forecast that recycled-content copper production will rise by more than 5 percent year on year in 2027.

By Brian Taylor, Senior Editor

Published April 28, 2026
Listen to article
/

The Lisbon-based International Copper Study Group (ICSG) has released a 2026 and 2027 forecast that predicts output in recycled-content copper considerably will outpace that of primary copper next year.

“Primary refined production is forecast to rise by 2.3 percent, and secondary refined production (from scrap) is expected to grow by 5.7 percent [in 2027],” writes ICSG in its Copper Market Forecast 2026/2027 document.

One year ago, in an earlier iteration of the same annual document, ICSG predicted 0.9 percent growth in overall refined copper production but a 6 percent increase in secondary refined production “as a consequence of new and expanded capacity in a number of countries.”

On the demand side, ICSG writes, “In view of uncertainty surrounding the conflict in the Middle East and disruption to trade flows, refined copper usage growth rates have been revised down compared to the group’s October 2025 forecast.”

ICSG says it now expects global refined copper use growth in 2026 of 1.6 percent (previously 2.1 percent). “In 2027, world refined copper usage is expected to grow by 2 percent,” the organization adds. 

Regarding the supply and demand balances that can help determine copper prices, ICSG has predicted a surplus of about 96,000 metric tons in 2026, which contrasts with a supply deficit of 150,000 metric tons forecast last October.

“This shift to surplus is attributed to lower than previously anticipated copper usage and higher secondary refined production,” according to the organization, which adds that it currently expects the market to create an even higher surplus of about 377,000 metric tons in 2027.
